question: Explain the Write-Through cache policy and state its main advantage.
expectedPoints: Write-Through updates both cache and main memory on every write
Main memory always has latest data
Simplifies cache coherency
Ensures data integrity for I/O devices

question: What is the purpose of the dirty bit in Write-Back cache? How is it used during cache line eviction?
expectedPoints: Dirty bit indicates whether cache line is modified
If dirty bit is set, data differs from main memory
Before eviction, dirty line must be written back
After write-back, dirty bit is cleared

question: Compare Write-Through and Write-Back policies in terms of write latency and bus traffic.
expectedPoints: Write-Through: higher latency (waits for memory)
Write-Back: lower latency (completes in cache)
Write-Through: higher bus traffic (every write)
Write-Back: lower bus traffic (only on evictions)

question: Explain write-allocate and no-write allocation policies used with write misses.
expectedPoints: Write-allocate: load block into cache then update
No-write allocation: write directly to memory, skip cache
Write-allocate used with Write-Back
No-write allocation used with Write-Through

question: What is cache pollution? How does write-around policy help prevent it?
expectedPoints: Cache pollution: useful data displaced by unnecessary data
Write-around: bypass cache on write miss
Data written once and rarely read stays out of cache
Improves cache hit rate for frequently accessed data

question: Describe the role of a write buffer in Write-Back cache systems.
expectedPoints: Write buffer temporarily stores data for write-back
Decouples CPU from slow main memory write
Allows CPU to continue execution faster
Data is written to memory asynchronously

question: Why is Write-Through preferred in multiprocessor systems?
expectedPoints: Main memory always has latest data
Easier to maintain cache coherency
Other processors see consistent data
No stale data issues between processors

question: Explain the Write-Back cache policy in detail. Include explanation of dirty bit, write-back process, and compare its performance characteristics with Write-Through.
expectedPoints: Write-Back updates only cache initially, memory updated on eviction
Dirty bit tracks modified lines
Write-back process: check dirty bit, write if set, then replace
Write-Back advantages: lower write latency, reduced bus traffic
Write-Back disadvantages: complex coherency, potential stale data
Suitable for write-intensive workloads
Write-Through advantages: simple coherency, data integrity
Write-Through disadvantages: higher latency, more traffic

question: Discuss the various write policies in cache memory: Write-Through, Write-Back, Write-Around. Explain when each is suitable with justification.
expectedPoints: Write-Through: both cache and memory updated simultaneously
Write-Back: cache updated first, memory on eviction
Write-Around: write misses bypass cache
Write-Through suitable: I/O devices, multiprocessors, data integrity priority
Write-Back suitable: single processor, performance priority, write-intensive
Write-Around suitable: streaming writes, one-time data access
Trade-offs: performance vs complexity vs coherency

question: Explain the concept of write miss in cache memory. Discuss how write misses are handled in (i) write-allocate and (ii) no-write allocation policies with their implications on cache performance.
expectedPoints: Write miss: address not found in cache on write
Write-allocate: fetch block from memory to cache, then write
No-write allocation: write directly to memory, don't load to cache
Write-allocate: subsequent writes to same block are faster
No-write allocation: avoids cache pollution
Write-allocate typically used with Write-Back
No-write allocation typically used with Write-Through
Performance impact on hit rate and bus traffic

question: Design a flowchart or algorithm for handling a write operation in a Write-Back cache. Include handling of write hits, write misses, and cache line eviction with dirty bit management.
expectedPoints: Check if address in cache (write hit or miss)
Write hit: update cache line, set dirty bit
Write miss: decide allocate policy
If write-allocate: fetch block, update, set dirty
On eviction: check dirty bit
If dirty: write back to memory
Then replace with new block
Clear dirty bit after write-back
